seoTitle: How to Remove or Change a Domain in Google Workspace
metaDescription: Find out how to remove a secondary domain from Google Workspace, handle users and DNS cleanup, and whether you can change your primary domain.
intro: Restructuring your organization's email setup sometimes means removing a domain you no longer need, or wondering whether you can swap your primary domain entirely. This guide walks through the correct way to delete secondary or unused domains in Google Workspace, including how to handle affected users, email aliases, and DNS cleanup. It also answers directly whether changing your primary domain is possible.

steps.heading: Decide which domain needs to be removed
steps.body: Confirm whether you're removing a secondary domain or trying to change the primary domain, since Google Workspace treats these differently.

steps.heading: Handle users tied to the domain
steps.body: Reassign or transfer users and email aliases connected to the domain before removing it, so nobody loses access unexpectedly.

steps.heading: Remove the domain in Admin Console
steps.body: Delete the secondary or unused domain through the Google Admin Console once affected users and aliases have been handled.

steps.heading: Clean up DNS records
steps.body: Update or remove the DNS records tied to the domain you removed to avoid leftover configuration issues.

faq.question: Can I remove a secondary domain from Google Workspace?
faq.answer: Yes, secondary or unused domains can be deleted through the Google Admin Console after reassigning affected users.

faq.question: Can you change the primary domain in Google Workspace?
faq.answer: Changing the primary domain directly isn't straightforward in Google Workspace, and the guide covers what to do if it isn't allowed.

faq.question: What happens to users when I remove a domain?
faq.answer: Users and email aliases tied to that domain need to be handled or transferred before the domain can be safely removed.

faq.question: Do I need to update DNS after removing a domain?
faq.answer: Yes, cleaning up the related DNS records helps avoid leftover configuration problems after the domain is removed.
